1: Chapter 27 introduces one of Jeremiah's most confrontational sign-acts: the yoke. In the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, this word came to Jeremiah from YHWH. There is a textual discrepancy: the narrative that follows (vv3, 12) refers to Zedekiah, not Jehoiakim. Most scholars conclude the chapter belongs to Zedekiah's reign (c. 594 BC, just after a coalition of western nations met to discuss rebellion against Babylon), and the Jehoiakim reference is a scribal error or an older heading that was not updated. The Zedekiah setting is confirmed by the parallel narrative in chapter 28.
2: Thus YHWH said to me: Make yourself straps and yoke bars, and put them on your neck. The yoke (ol) is a wooden frame fitting over the neck and shoulders of a draft animal, used to harness it to a plow or wagon. Wearing a yoke publicly was the ultimate symbol of subjugation — a human being reduced to the status of a work animal under a master's authority. Jeremiah is commanded to wear this yoke as he delivers his message to foreign ambassadors, making the physical object an extension of the spoken oracle.
3: Send word to the king of Edom, the king of Moab, the king of the sons of Ammon, the king of Tyre, and the king of Sidon by the hand of the envoys who have come to Jerusalem to Zedekiah king of Judah. The historical occasion: representatives of Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, and Sidon had gathered in Jerusalem — likely to discuss a coordinated rebellion against Babylonian control. Jeremiah sends yoke-messages to all five kings through their own ambassadors: they should each receive not just a verbal message but an additional symbolic object — a yoke of their own.
4: Give them this charge for their masters: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: this is what you shall say to your masters. The message is delivered by Jeremiah but addressed to the kings through their envoys. The political audacity is remarkable: Jeremiah is effectively counter-lobbying the diplomatic conference that had gathered in Jerusalem to plan resistance to Babylon. His counter-message is the theological opposite of what the coalition was there to promote.
5: It is I who by my great power and my outstretched arm have made the earth, with the men and animals that are on the earth, and I give it to whoever it seems right to me. The creation-sovereignty claim grounds the political message: YHWH made the earth and all its inhabitants — human and animal. The sovereign creator has the right to allocate political dominion as he wills. Outstretched arm (zero'i netuyah) is the Exodus-war language (Exodus 6:6; Deuteronomy 4:34) — the same power that liberated Israel from Egypt now assigns the earth's nations to Nebuchadnezzar's governance.
6: Now I have given all these lands into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar, the king of Babylon, my servant, and I have given him also the beasts of the field to serve him. The my servant designation for Nebuchadnezzar reappears from Jeremiah 25:9. The addition of the beasts of the field to serve him extends the sovereignty claim: even the wild animals of the region are under Nebuchadnezzar's providential authority during this period. The theological claim is staggering in its scope: YHWH has assigned world dominion to a pagan king as an act of divine sovereignty.
7: All the nations shall serve him and his son and his grandson, until the time of his own land comes. Then many nations and great kings shall make him their slave. The temporal limit on Babylonian dominion: three generations (Nebuchadnezzar, his son, his grandson) — then Babylon itself will be subjugated. The historical fulfillment came with the Persian conquest of Babylon in 539 BC under Cyrus. The theological logic is consistent: Babylon is a temporary instrument of YHWH's judgment, not a permanent world empire. All human empires operate within YHWH's permissive will for a limited period.
8: But if any nation or kingdom will not serve this Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and put its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon, I will punish that nation with the sword, with famine, and with pestilence, declares YHWH, until I have consumed it by his hand. Resistance to Babylon is resistance to YHWH's appointed order for this historical period. The judgment triad (sword, famine, pestilence) applies to nations that resist — using the same instruments YHWH uses to judge Judah. The political counsel to submit to Babylon is thus simultaneously covenant theology: accept YHWH's judgment as the path to survival.
9: So do not listen to your prophets, your diviners, your dreamers, your fortune-tellers, or your sorcerers, who are saying to you, You shall not serve the king of Babylon. The false prophets of the surrounding nations are giving the same message as Judah's false prophets: do not submit to Babylon. The entire prophetic establishment of the region is aligning against Jeremiah's counter-message. Diviners, dreamers, fortune-tellers, and sorcerers — the full range of ANE prophetic and mantic practices — are all delivering the politically comfortable message of resistance.
10: For it is a lie that they are prophesying to you, with the result that you will be removed far from your land, and I will drive you out, and you will perish. The false prophets' message is a lie (sheqer) — not merely incorrect information but a deliberate deception that leads to destruction. The nations that trust the false prophets and resist Babylon will be removed from their lands. The specific threat of removal from the land applies to Gentile nations in the same way it applies to Israel: the connection between covenant faithfulness and land-tenure operates across national boundaries.
11: But any nation that will bring its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon and serve him, I will leave on its own land, to work it and dwell there, declares YHWH. Submission to Babylon guarantees land-retention. The paradox is complete: what appears to be humiliating defeat (wearing the yoke) is actually the path to survival and continued possession of the land. What appears to be honorable resistance is actually the path to destruction and exile. YHWH's ways are not the ways of human political logic.
12: To Zedekiah king of Judah I spoke in like manner: Bring your necks under the yoke of the king of Babylon and serve him and his people and live. The message to Zedekiah is identical to the message to the foreign kings: submit and live. The political theology applies to the Davidic king as much as to the pagan rulers surrounding him. Zedekiah repeatedly consulted Jeremiah (Jeremiah 21, 34, 37, 38) but ultimately could not bring himself to surrender — partly from fear of the Judaean anti-Babylonian faction (Jeremiah 38:19). His inability to act on Jeremiah's counsel sealed Jerusalem's fate.
13: Why will you and your people die by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence, as YHWH has spoken concerning any nation that will not serve the king of Babylon? The rhetorical question pressing Zedekiah toward action: the three instruments of judgment are preventable. Submission averts them; resistance guarantees them. The simplicity of the choice makes the failure to choose wisely all the more tragic.
14: Do not listen to the words of the prophets who are saying to you, You shall not serve the king of Babylon, for it is a lie that they are prophesying to you. The false prophets in Zedekiah's court are telling him what political logic and national pride demand: resist, do not submit. Jeremiah identifies their message as sheqer (lie) — not merely a different political analysis but a false theological claim about YHWH's intentions.
15: I did not send them, declares YHWH, but they are prophesying falsely in my name, with the result that I will drive you out and you will perish, you and the prophets who are prophesying to you. The I did not send formula is the definitive criterion: unauthorized speech in YHWH's name is false prophecy regardless of its political popularity. The consequence — I will drive you out, you will perish — applies to both the king who follows the false counsel and the prophets who gave it. False prophecy is not a victimless crime; it destroys both the deceiver and the deceived.
16: Then I spoke to the priests and to all this people, saying, Thus says YHWH: Do not listen to the words of your prophets who are prophesying to you, saying, Behold, the vessels of YHWH's house will now shortly be brought back from Babylon, for it is a lie that they are prophesying to you. The Temple vessels had been taken to Babylon in 597 BC when Jehoiachin was deported (2 Kings 24:13). The false prophets were promising a quick return of these sacred objects — a claim that would have been politically and religiously popular, suggesting that the exile was temporary and YHWH's honor would soon be restored. Jeremiah declares this claim a lie.
17: Do not listen to them; serve the king of Babylon and live. Why should this city become a desolation? The pastoral urgency of Jeremiah's message: why should this city become a desolation? He does not want Jerusalem destroyed — he weeps for it (Jeremiah 9:1; Jeremiah 14:17). The call to submit to Babylon is not treachery but compassion: the prophet who prophesies surrender is the one trying to prevent the city's destruction, while the patriots who resist are ensuring it.
18: If they are prophets, and if the word of YHWH is with them, then let them intercede with YHWH of hosts, that the vessels that are left in the house of YHWH, in the house of the king of Judah, and in Jerusalem may not go to Babylon. Jeremiah challenges the false prophets: if you genuinely have access to YHWH's word, use it to intercede for the remaining Temple vessels. This is a practical test of prophetic authenticity: the true prophet can intercede effectively (cf. Moses, Samuel); the false prophet's intercession is empty. The challenge presupposes that Jeremiah knows the intercession will fail — the remaining vessels are going to Babylon too.
19: For thus says YHWH of hosts concerning the pillars, the sea, the stands, and the rest of the vessels that are left in this city. The specific Temple furnishings are named: the bronze pillars Jachin and Boaz (1 Kings 7:15–22), the molten sea (the great bronze basin, 1 Kings 7:23–26), and the bronze stands (1 Kings 7:27–39). These objects taken to Babylon in 597 were the largest and most significant Temple furnishings; the ones remaining are also important. Jeremiah's specificity underlines the certainty of what he is predicting.
20: Which Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon did not take away, when he took into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon Jeconiah the son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, and all the nobles of Judah and Jerusalem. The historical specification: the 597 BC deportation took some vessels but left others. The false prophets pointed to the remaining vessels as evidence that YHWH was still protecting Jerusalem's sacred objects. Jeremiah declares that the remaining vessels are still in Jerusalem not because of YHWH's protection but because Babylon has not yet come for them.
21: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, concerning the vessels that are left in the house of YHWH, in the house of the king of Judah, and in Jerusalem. The oracle concerning the remaining vessels is delivered with formal authority: the full divine title YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel. The solemnity signals the importance of what follows.
22: They shall be carried to Babylon and remain there until the day when I visit them, declares YHWH. Then I will bring them back and restore them to this place. The oracle is both devastating and hopeful: the remaining vessels will go to Babylon — confirming Jeremiah's message against the false prophets — but they will return when YHWH visits (paqad) them. The same verb used for divine judgment (visitation for punishment) here means visitation for restoration. The exile of the Temple vessels anticipates the exile of the people; the return of the vessels anticipates the return of the people. Historically, the Temple vessels were returned under Cyrus in 538 BC (Ezra 1:7–11), just as Jeremiah predicted.
